mappings.rationale: Covers the European partition of Africa, the Berlin West Africa Conference, and the establishment of European colonies across sub-Saharan Africa from 1885 to 1920.
The imperialistic scramble for African colonies, Ottoman decline, and European penetration of Egypt, the Maghrib, and the Arabian Peninsula exemplify imperial expansion.
British rule in Egypt (1882-1922), the Anglo-Egyptian Condominium in Sudan, and British colonial activity in East and West Africa are central to this section.
Arab nationalism from c. 1850 to 1920, Turkish nationalism under Abdulhamid II, and Egyptian nationalism are major themes of this section.
The Ottoman Empire, the Mahdist movement in Sudan, and Islamic dimensions of Arab nationalism are significant topics in this section.
The decline of the Ottoman Empire, the Tanzimat reforms, and the development of Arab nationalism are central episodes in Islamic history.
The emergence of Zionism, Jewish immigration to Palestine after 1880, and the beginning of conflict between Zionists and Arab nationalists are explicitly discussed.
The origins of the Zionist movement, Jewish immigration to Palestine, and World War I developments including the Balfour Declaration are directly covered.
Iran under the Qajar dynasty from 1779 to 1925, including European penetration by British and Russians, is explicitly covered in this section.
The establishment of European colonial regimes in Africa and the development of indirect rule set the stage for later decolonization movements.
The European partition of Africa and colonial administration provide the historical foundation that postcolonial theory critiques.
French colonial expansion into Algeria, Tunisia, Morocco, and sub-Saharan Africa, including French Congo policies, reflects France's imperial ambitions.
The Berlin West Africa Conference, European diplomatic rivalry over African colonies, and World War I settlements involve significant diplomatic history.
The origins of the Zionist movement were partly a response to European antisemitism, providing context for Jewish immigration to Palestine discussed in this section.
The Zionist movement and Jewish immigration to Palestine after 1880 are rooted in Jewish religious and cultural identity.
The interaction between European Christian missionaries and indigenous African religious practices during the colonial period is relevant.
European exploratory activities in the 19th century, including Livingstone and Stanley's expeditions in Central Africa, are explicitly mentioned.
The European scramble for Africa, the partition at the Berlin Conference, and Great Power rivalries in the Ottoman domains reflect geopolitical dynamics.
European intervention in the Ottoman Empire, the partition of Africa, and wartime diplomacy reshaped international relations in Southwest Asia and Africa.
The establishment of European colonial regimes, development of indirect rule, and the consolidation of Ethiopian central government shaped African political structures.
The Mahdist movement in Sudan, Ottoman pan-Islamism, and the emergence of Arab nationalism have global Islamic dimensions.
The Young Turk movement and revolutionary nationalism in the Ottoman Empire involved forms of political violence relevant to the study of terrorism.
